title: Spaced Retrieval / Distributed Practice
anchor_citations: Cepeda, N. J., Pashler, H., Vul, E., Wixted, J. T., & Rohrer, D. (2006). Distributed practice in verbal recall tasks: A review and quantitative synthesis. Psychological Bulletin, 132(3), 354-380. doi:10.1037/0033-2909.132.3.354

title: Interleaving
anchor_citations: Brunmair, M., & Richter, T. (2019). Similarity matters: A meta-analysis of interleaved learning and its moderators. Psychological Bulletin, 145(11), 1029-1052. doi:10.1037/bul0000209
Rohrer, D., & Taylor, K. (2007). The shuffling of mathematics problems improves learning. Instructional Science, 35(6), 481-498. doi:10.1007/s11251-007-9015-8

title: Worked Example Effect
anchor_citations: Sweller, J., & Cooper, G. A. (1985). The use of worked examples as a substitute for problem solving in learning algebra. Cognition and Instruction, 2(1), 59-89. doi:10.1207/s1532690xci0201_3
Renkl, A. (2014). Toward an instructionally oriented theory of example-based learning. Cognitive Science, 38(1), 1-37. doi:10.1111/cogs.12086
notes: Pair this chapter with expertise-reversal — worked examples are the classic novice intervention that reverses at expertise threshold.

title: Expertise Reversal Effect
anchor_citations: Kalyuga, S., Ayres, P., Chandler, P., & Sweller, J. (2003). The expertise reversal effect. Educational Psychologist, 38(1), 23-31. doi:10.1207/S15326985EP3801_4
notes: This is a paired-with-worked-example-effect chapter; emphasize the reversal at expertise threshold (~70% mastery on a competency test).

title: Cognitive Load Theory
anchor_citations: Sweller, J. (1988). Cognitive load during problem solving: Effects on learning. Cognitive Science, 12(2), 257-285. doi:10.1207/s15516709cog1202_4
Sweller, J., Ayres, P., & Kalyuga, S. (2011). Cognitive load theory. New York: Springer. doi:10.1007/978-1-4419-8126-4
notes: Foundational theoretical framework; effect_size may be null with a justification comment (no single d summarizes the construct).

title: Pretesting Effect
anchor_citations: Carpenter, S. K., & Toftness, A. R. (2017). The effect of prequestions on learning from video presentations. Journal of Applied Research in Memory and Cognition, 6(1), 104-109. doi:10.1016/j.jarmac.2016.07.014

Pan, S. C., & Sana, F. (2021). Pretesting versus posttesting: Comparing the pedagogical benefits of errorful generation and retrieval practice. Journal of Experimental Psychology: Applied, 27(2), 237-257. doi:10.1037/xap0000345
notes: Distinct from the testing effect; pretesting precedes encoding while retrieval practice follows it. Cross-reference but do not conflate.

title: Desirable Difficulties
anchor_citations: Bjork, E. L., & Bjork, R. A. (2011). Making things hard on yourself, but in a good way: Creating desirable difficulties to enhance learning. In M. A. Gernsbacher, R. W. Pew, L. M. Hough, & J. R. Pomerantz (Eds.), Psychology and the real world: Essays illustrating fundamental contributions to society (pp. 56-64). New York: Worth.
notes: Foundational umbrella concept; effect_size may be null with justification comment. Cross-reference all the specific instances (testing-effect, spaced-retrieval, interleaving, generation effect).

title: Forgetting Curve & Relearning
anchor_citations: Murre, J. M. J., & Dros, J. (2015). Replication and analysis of Ebbinghaus' forgetting curve. PLoS ONE, 10(7), e0120644. doi:10.1371/journal.pone.0120644
Ebbinghaus, H. (1885/1913). Memory: A contribution to experimental psychology (H. A. Ruger & C. E. Bussenius, Trans.). New York: Teachers College, Columbia University.
notes: Foundational; effect_size may be null with justification comment. Murre & Dros (2015) is the modern replication that bears on the current evidence base.

title: Dual Coding
anchor_citations: Paivio, A. (1991). Dual coding theory: Retrospect and current status. Canadian Journal of Psychology, 45(3), 255-287. doi:10.1037/h0084295
Mayer, R. E. (2014). The Cambridge handbook of multimedia learning (2nd ed.). Cambridge: Cambridge University Press. doi:10.1017/CBO9781139547369

title: Self-Explanation & Elaborative Interrogation
anchor_citations: Chi, M. T. H., De Leeuw, N., Chiu, M.-H., & LaVancher, C. (1994). Eliciting self-explanations improves understanding. Cognitive Science, 18(3), 439-477. doi:10.1207/s15516709cog1803_3

Dunlosky, J., Rawson, K. A., Marsh, E. J., Nathan, M. J., & Willingham, D. T. (2013). Improving students' learning with effective learning techniques: Promising directions from cognitive and educational psychology. Psychological Science in the Public Interest, 14(1), 4-58. doi:10.1177/1529100612453266
notes: Related to but distinct from self-explanation-prompts (which is the delivery-pattern operationalization). This chapter is the underlying learning-science principle; the delivery chapter is the move.

title: Schema Theory & Knowledge Components
anchor_citations: Anderson, R. C. (1977). The notion of schemata and the educational enterprise. In R. C. Anderson, R. J. Spiro, & W. E. Montague (Eds.), Schooling and the acquisition of knowledge (pp. 415-431). Hillsdale, NJ: Erlbaum.

Koedinger, K. R., Corbett, A. T., & Perfetti, C. (2012). The knowledge-learning-instruction framework: Bridging the science-practice chasm to enhance robust student learning. Cognitive Science, 36(5), 757-798. doi:10.1111/j.1551-6709.2012.01245.x
notes: Foundational theoretical framework; effect_size may be null with justification comment. The KC (knowledge component) decomposition from Koedinger is the operational form most useful at runtime.

title: Transfer of Learning
anchor_citations: Barnett, S. M., & Ceci, S. J. (2002). When and where do we apply what we learn? A taxonomy for far transfer. Psychological Bulletin, 128(4), 612-637. doi:10.1037/0033-2909.128.4.612
notes: Foundational; effect_size may be null with justification comment. The Barnett & Ceci taxonomy is the operational backbone — near vs. far transfer along the nine dimensions they specify.

title: Backward Design (UbD)
anchor_citations: Wiggins, G., & McTighe, J. (2005). Understanding by design (Expanded 2nd ed.). Alexandria, VA: ASCD.
notes: Foundational design framework; effect_size null with justification comment. The three-stage backward sequence (desired results → acceptable evidence → learning experiences) is the operational form.

title: Bloom's Revised Taxonomy
anchor_citations: Anderson, L. W., & Krathwohl, D. R. (Eds.). (2001). A taxonomy for learning, teaching, and assessing: A revision of Bloom's taxonomy of educational objectives. New York: Longman.
notes: Foundational taxonomy; effect_size null with justification comment. The two-dimensional knowledge x cognitive-process matrix is the operational form.

title: Merrill's First Principles
anchor_citations: Merrill, M. D. (2002). First principles of instruction. Educational Technology Research and Development, 50(3), 43-59. doi:10.1007/BF02505024
Merrill, M. D. (2013). First principles of instruction: Identifying and designing effective, efficient, and engaging instruction. San Francisco: Pfeiffer.

title: Gagné's Nine Events
anchor_citations: Gagné, R. M., Briggs, L. J., & Wager, W. W. (1992). Principles of instructional design (4th ed.). Fort Worth: Harcourt Brace Jovanovich.
notes: Foundational sequence framework; effect_size null with justification comment.

title: 5E Learning Cycle
anchor_citations: Bybee, R. W., Taylor, J. A., Gardner, A., Van Scotter, P., Powell, J. C., Westbrook, A., & Landes, N. (2006). The BSCS 5E instructional model: Origins and effectiveness. Colorado Springs, CO: BSCS.
notes: Foundational design pattern; effect_size null with justification comment. The five phases (Engage, Explore, Explain, Elaborate, Evaluate) are the operational form.

title: Mastery Learning
anchor_citations: Guskey, T. R. (2010). Lessons of mastery learning. Educational Leadership, 68(2), 52-57.
Bloom, B. S. (1968). Learning for mastery. Evaluation Comment, 1(2), 1-12.
notes: Distinguish from the Bloom 2-sigma claim (excluded). Guskey 2010 is the more conservative modern estimate (d ≈ 0.5).

title: 4C/ID Model
anchor_citations: van Merriënboer, J. J. G., & Kirschner, P. A. (2018). Ten steps to complex learning: A systematic approach to four-component instructional design (3rd ed.). New York: Routledge. doi:10.4324/9780203096864
notes: Foundational design framework for complex skills; effect_size null with justification comment.

title: Item-Writing Rules (Haladyna + Shank)
anchor_citations: Haladyna, T. M., Downing, S. M., & Rodriguez, M. C. (2002). A review of multiple-choice item-writing guidelines for classroom assessment. Applied Measurement in Education, 15(3), 309-333. doi:10.1207/S15324818AME1503_5
Shank, P. (2014). Write better multiple-choice questions to assess learning: Measure what matters. Learning Peaks LLC.
notes: Use measurement values in applies_to. This is the operational rules chapter for MCQ construction.

title: Bloom Levels in Assessment
anchor_citations: Krathwohl, D. R. (2002). A revision of Bloom's taxonomy: An overview. Theory Into Practice, 41(4), 212-218. doi:10.1207/s15430421tip4104_2
notes: Use measurement values in applies_to. effect_size null with justification (this is a classification framework, not an effect-producing intervention).

title: Distractor Analysis
anchor_citations: Haladyna, T. M., & Rodriguez, M. C. (2013). Developing and validating test items. New York: Routledge. doi:10.4324/9780203850381
notes: Use measurement values in applies_to. effect_size null with justification (this is an analytic technique).

title: Pre/Post Assessment & Effect Size
anchor_citations: Hattie, J. (2009). Visible learning: A synthesis of over 800 meta-analyses relating to achievement. London: Routledge. doi:10.4324/9780203887332
Cohen, J. (1988). Statistical power analysis for the behavioral sciences (2nd ed.). Hillsdale, NJ: Erlbaum.
notes: Use measurement values in applies_to. This chapter is the methodology backbone for measuring whether the tutor is producing learning.

title: Validity & Reliability
anchor_citations: Cronbach, L. J. (1951). Coefficient alpha and the internal structure of tests. Psychometrika, 16(3), 297-334. doi:10.1007/BF02310555
Messick, S. (1989). Validity. In R. L. Linn (Ed.), Educational measurement (3rd ed., pp. 13-103). New York: Macmillan.
notes: Use measurement values in applies_to. effect_size null with justification (foundational psychometric framework).

title: Mastery Threshold & Transfer Test Design
anchor_citations: Bloom, B. S. (1968). Learning for mastery. Evaluation Comment, 1(2), 1-12.
Barnett, S. M., & Ceci, S. J. (2002). When and where do we apply what we learn? A taxonomy for far transfer. Psychological Bulletin, 128(4), 612-637. doi:10.1037/0033-2909.128.4.612
notes: Use measurement and decision values in applies_to. This chapter operationalizes how to set the mastery cutoff and how to write transfer items.

title: Socratic Questioning
anchor_citations: Chi, M. T. H. (2009). Active-constructive-interactive: A conceptual framework for differentiating learning activities. Topics in Cognitive Science, 1(1), 73-105. doi:10.1111/j.1756-8765.2008.01005.x
Paul, R., & Elder, L. (2007). Critical thinking: The art of Socratic questioning. Journal of Developmental Education, 31(1), 36-37.

title: Predict-Before-Reveal
anchor_citations: Carpenter, S. K., & Toftness, A. R. (2017). The effect of prequestions on learning from video presentations. Journal of Applied Research in Memory and Cognition, 6(1), 104-109. doi:10.1016/j.jarmac.2016.07.014
notes: This is the delivery-pattern operationalization of pretesting and retrieval. Cross-reference both.

title: Faded Worked Examples
anchor_citations: Renkl, A., & Atkinson, R. K. (2003). Structuring the transition from example study to problem solving in cognitive skill acquisition: A cognitive load perspective. Educational Psychologist, 38(1), 15-22. doi:10.1207/S15326985EP3801_3
notes: Delivery-pattern operationalization of the worked-example-effect across the expertise gradient. Cross-reference worked-example-effect and expertise-reversal.

title: Self-Explanation Prompts
anchor_citations: Chi, M. T. H., De Leeuw, N., Chiu, M.-H., & LaVancher, C. (1994). Eliciting self-explanations improves understanding. Cognitive Science, 18(3), 439-477. doi:10.1207/s15516709cog1803_3
notes: This is the delivery-pattern chapter for what self-explanation-elaborative-interrogation describes as principle. Focus on the prompt-design moves.

title: Concept Mapping
anchor_citations: Nesbit, J. C., & Adesope, O. O. (2006). Learning with concept and knowledge maps: A meta-analysis. Review of Educational Research, 76(3), 413-448. doi:10.3102/00346543076003413

Novak, J. D., & Cañas, A. J. (2008). The theory underlying concept maps and how to construct and use them. Technical Report IHMC CmapTools 2006-01 Rev 01-2008. Florida Institute for Human and Machine Cognition.

title: Error Analysis & Corrective Feedback
anchor_citations: Hattie, J., & Timperley, H. (2007). The power of feedback. Review of Educational Research, 77(1), 81-112. doi:10.3102/003465430298487

title: Analogical Bridging
anchor_citations: Gentner, D. (1983). Structure-mapping: A theoretical framework for analogy. Cognitive Science, 7(2), 155-170. doi:10.1207/s15516709cog0702_3
Holyoak, K. J. (2012). Analogy and relational reasoning. In K. J. Holyoak & R. G. Morrison (Eds.), The Oxford handbook of thinking and reasoning (pp. 234-259). New York: Oxford University Press.

title: Productive Failure
anchor_citations: Kapur, M. (2008). Productive failure. Cognition and Instruction, 26(3), 379-424. doi:10.1080/07370000802212669
Kapur, M. (2014). Productive failure in learning math. Cognitive Science, 38(5), 1008-1022. doi:10.1111/cogs.12107
notes: Boundary condition matters here: productive failure works when learners have the prerequisite knowledge to generate solutions but lack the canonical method. Spell out the boundary explicitly.

title: Cognitive Apprenticeship Mentor
anchor_citations: Collins, A., Brown, J. S., & Newman, S. E. (1989). Cognitive apprenticeship: Teaching the crafts of reading, writing, and mathematics. In L. B. Resnick (Ed.), Knowing, learning, and instruction: Essays in honor of Robert Glaser (pp. 453-494). Hillsdale, NJ: Erlbaum.
notes: Tutor-persona chapter; effect_size null with justification (persona is a stance, not an intervention with a single effect size). Cite the foundational framework but ground the operational moves (modeling, coaching, scaffolding, articulation, reflection, exploration) in the more recent literature.

title: Socratic Interlocutor
anchor_citations: Paul, R., & Elder, L. (2007). Critical thinking: The art of Socratic questioning. Journal of Developmental Education, 31(1), 36-37.
notes: Tutor-persona chapter (distinct from delivery-pattern socratic-questioning, which is the move set). Persona is the voice and stance the tutor adopts; questioning is one of the moves.

title: Coach-Encourager
anchor_citations: Hattie, J., & Timperley, H. (2007). The power of feedback. Review of Educational Research, 77(1), 81-112. doi:10.3102/003465430298487

Kluger, A. N., & DeNisi, A. (1996). The effects of feedback interventions on performance: A historical review, a meta-analysis, and a preliminary feedback intervention theory. Psychological Bulletin, 119(2), 254-284. doi:10.1037/0033-2909.119.2.254
notes: Tutor-persona chapter; effect_size null with justification. Pull from the feedback meta-analyses (Kluger & DeNisi 1996 found that ~38% of feedback interventions actually decreased performance — the persona has to know how to encourage without triggering the demotivation pattern).

title: Voice Style Guide & Agency-Preserving Language
anchor_citations: Reeves, B., & Nass, C. (1996). The media equation: How people treat computers, television, and new media like real people and places. Cambridge: Cambridge University Press.
notes: Tutor-persona chapter; effect_size null with justification. The Reeves & Nass finding (people apply social rules to computers) is the foundational finding; chapter operationalizes specific phrasing patterns that preserve learner agency (asking vs. telling, naming the move, surfacing the choice).

title: Self-Determination Theory
anchor_citations: Deci, E. L., & Ryan, R. M. (2000). The 'what' and 'why' of goal pursuits: Human needs and the self-determination of behavior. Psychological Inquiry, 11(4), 227-268. doi:10.1207/S15327965PLI1104_01
notes: Foundational motivation framework; effect_size null with justification. The three needs (autonomy, competence, relatedness) are the operational form for tutor moves.

title: Self-Efficacy
anchor_citations: Bandura, A. (1977). Self-efficacy: Toward a unifying theory of behavioral change. Psychological Review, 84(2), 191-215. doi:10.1037/0033-295X.84.2.191
Bandura, A. (1997). Self-efficacy: The exercise of control. New York: W. H. Freeman.

title: Goal-Setting Theory
anchor_citations: Locke, E. A., & Latham, G. P. (2002). Building a practically useful theory of goal setting and task motivation: A 35-year odyssey. American Psychologist, 57(9), 705-717. doi:10.1037/0003-066X.57.9.705

title: Flow Theory
anchor_citations: Nakamura, J., & Csikszentmihalyi, M. (2014). The concept of flow. In M. Csikszentmihalyi (Ed.), Flow and the foundations of positive psychology (pp. 239-263). Dordrecht: Springer. doi:10.1007/978-94-017-9088-8_16
Csikszentmihalyi, M. (1990). Flow: The psychology of optimal experience. New York: Harper & Row.
notes: Foundational construct; effect_size null with justification. Operationally the chapter is about the challenge-skill balance — keeping the task at the edge of current competence.

title: Achievement Goal Theory
anchor_citations: Senko, C., & Hulleman, C. S. (2013). The role of goal attainment expectancies in achievement goal pursuit. Journal of Educational Psychology, 105(2), 504-521. doi:10.1037/a0031136
Ames, C. (1992). Classrooms: Goals, structures, and student motivation. Journal of Educational Psychology, 84(3), 261-271. doi:10.1037/0022-0663.84.3.261
notes: Operationally, the chapter is about mastery-goal vs. performance-goal framing of feedback and assessment.

title: Bayesian Knowledge Tracing
anchor_citations: Corbett, A. T., & Anderson, J. R. (1995). Knowledge tracing: Modeling the acquisition of procedural knowledge. User Modeling and User-Adapted Interaction, 4(4), 253-278. doi:10.1007/BF01099821
notes: Use measurement, sequencing, and decision values in applies_to. effect_size null with justification (this is an algorithm, not an intervention with a single d). Spell out the four parameters (P(L0), P(T), P(S), P(G)) and where the runtime gets each.

title: Performance Factor Analysis
anchor_citations: Pavlik, P. I., Cen, H., & Koedinger, K. R. (2009). Performance factors analysis - A new alternative to knowledge tracing. In Proceedings of the 14th International Conference on Artificial Intelligence in Education (pp. 531-538). Amsterdam: IOS Press.
notes: Use measurement, sequencing, and decision values in applies_to. effect_size null with justification (algorithm). Position relative to BKT — PFA is the logistic-regression alternative that uses success and failure counts on knowledge components rather than a hidden-state Bayesian model.

title: SM-2 / FSRS
anchor_citations: Wozniak, P. A., & Gorzelanczyk, E. J. (1994). Optimization of repetition spacing in the practice of learning. Acta Neurobiologiae Experimentalis, 54(1), 59-62.

Ye, J., Su, J., & Cao, Y. (2022). A stochastic shortest path algorithm for optimizing spaced repetition scheduling. In Proceedings of the 28th ACM SIGKDD Conference on Knowledge Discovery and Data Mining (pp. 4381-4390). New York: ACM. doi:10.1145/3534678.3539081
notes: Use sequencing and decision values in applies_to. effect_size null with justification (these are scheduling algorithms). FSRS (Free Spaced Repetition Scheduler) is the modern open-source successor; SM-2 is the SuperMemo lineage. Cross-reference spaced-retrieval for the underlying principle.

title: Knowledge Graph Traversal
anchor_citations: Koedinger, K. R., Corbett, A. T., & Perfetti, C. (2012). The knowledge-learning-instruction framework: Bridging the science-practice chasm to enhance robust student learning. Cognitive Science, 36(5), 757-798. doi:10.1111/j.1551-6709.2012.01245.x
notes: Use sequencing and decision values in applies_to. effect_size null with justification (this is a graph-search method, not an intervention). Cross-reference schema-theory-knowledge-components for the underlying KC decomposition this traverses.

title: ZPD Operationalization
anchor_citations: Vygotsky, L. S. (1978). Mind in society: The development of higher psychological processes (M. Cole, V. John-Steiner, S. Scribner, & E. Souberman, Eds.). Cambridge, MA: Harvard University Press.
notes: Use sequencing and decision values in applies_to. effect_size null with justification. The chapter operationalizes the ZPD as a target accuracy band (e.g., 70-85% on the next item) — Vygotsky is foundational but the runtime translation is what matters here.

title: Mastery Thresholds
anchor_citations: Bloom, B. S. (1968). Learning for mastery. Evaluation Comment, 1(2), 1-12.
Guskey, T. R. (2010). Lessons of mastery learning. Educational Leadership, 68(2), 52-57.
notes: Use decision values in applies_to. Distinct from mastery-learning (the design framework) and mastery-threshold-transfer-test-design (the test-construction chapter); this is the runtime-decision chapter on what cutoff to use.

title: Item Difficulty & Discrimination
anchor_citations: Lord, F. M. (1980). Applications of item response theory to practical testing problems. Hillsdale, NJ: Erlbaum.
Embretson, S. E., & Reise, S. P. (2000). Item response theory for psychologists. Mahwah, NJ: Erlbaum.
notes: Use measurement and decision values in applies_to. effect_size null with justification (psychometric framework). The chapter operationalizes the b and a parameters from IRT and how the runtime uses them to pick the next item.
